current_focus: Treat K=128 only as the initial search anchor, not as a success criterion. The research objective is to find configurations whose FVU is dramatically better than the current K=128 baseline, with a target on the order of halving that baseline FVU before rewarding smaller K or lower cost.

recent_results.description: Run a proxy mainline TopK anchor at K=128 using Adam and a reduced LR, keeping architecture and sparsity fixed so the round cleanly tests whether optimizer retuning materially lowers FVU.
Retry the same K=128 TopK Adam anchor after a narrow startup fix in `sparsify/__main__.py` that skips unnecessary rank-by-rank artifact loading when the model and dataset paths are local.
Retry the same K=128 TopK Adam anchor after a narrow startup fix in `sparsify/__main__.py` that skips unnecessary rank-by-rank artifact loading when the model and dataset paths are local. full

action.hypothesis: At fixed K=128, the current default launch is likely signum-tuned; switching the mainline TopK SAE to Adam with a lower learning rate should establish a stronger quality anchor before spending rounds on lower-K or new families.
action.summary: Run a proxy mainline TopK anchor at K=128 using Adam and a reduced LR, keeping architecture and sparsity fixed so the round cleanly tests whether optimizer retuning materially lowers FVU.
action.self_review: This is still a local optimization pass, so the gain may be limited if the quality ceiling is architectural rather than optimizer-driven. I am assuming the default 8e-4 is better suited to signum than Adam.
notes_to_memory: Round 0 should establish a trustworthy K=128 quality anchor before rewarding smaller K.
Use Adam with a lower LR as the first clean mainline retune; do not mix in lower-K objectives yet.
next_hypotheses: If Adam improves the K=128 anchor but remains far from the aggressive FVU target, add a quality-focused regularizer such as ortho_lambda at K=128.
If the Adam retune does not materially improve K=128 TopK quality, escalate quickly to a new encoder family rather than continuing weak local search.
If the proxy run is promising but slow, treat that as an implementation/perf follow-up rather than an architecture rejection.

action.hypothesis: The round-1 crash was a startup-latency issue rather than a verdict on K=128 TopK quality; the most likely blocker is heavy launch overhead from compiling all transformer layers before the first step, so disabling compile and reducing preprocessing worker fanout should get a clean proxy measurement.
action.summary: Retry the K=128 TopK Adam anchor as a proxy, but switch off model compilation and lower dataset preprocessing parallelism so the run can reach first metrics before the watchdog fires.
action.self_review: This does not improve quality directly and may leave some steady-state throughput on the table. If the run still times out before first metrics, the real bottleneck is likely elsewhere in startup rather than compile overhead.
notes_to_memory: Round 1 produced a first_step_timeout crash before any quality signal, so do not draw a negative conclusion about Adam or TopK from it.
Treat this as a startup/perf unblock attempt to recover a trustworthy K=128 anchor measurement.
next_hypotheses: If the retry runs and improves FVU, continue K=128 quality work before considering any lower-K point.
If the retry runs but quality remains weak, escalate to a new encoder family rather than continuing optimizer-local search.
If first_step_timeout persists even with compile disabled, investigate startup bottlenecks in sparsify/ for model loading, hook registration, or dataloader setup.

action.hypothesis: The first-step timeout is caused by serialized DDP startup for local artifacts rather than by the K=128 TopK/Adam configuration itself; allowing both ranks to load the local model and dataset in parallel should get the proxy run to first metrics.
action.summary: Retry the same K=128 TopK Adam anchor after a narrow startup fix in `sparsify/__main__.py` that skips unnecessary rank-by-rank artifact loading when the model and dataset paths are local.
action.self_review: This targets startup latency only, so it may not help if the watchdog is being tripped by a different pre-step bottleneck such as dataset shuffling or hook initialization. The quality hypothesis itself remains untested until the run reaches metrics.
notes_to_memory: Round 1 crash still has no usable FVU signal; treat it as startup-path failure, not as evidence against TopK or Adam.
Local DDP artifact loading was serialized in sparsify/__main__.py; this round parallelizes local-path startup across ranks to reduce first-step latency.
next_hypotheses: If the retry reaches metrics, resume K=128 quality work before considering any lower-K point.
If startup still times out, inspect dataset shuffle/select ordering and other pre-step costs in the local artifact path.
If the run succeeds but Adam does not materially improve K=128 quality, escalate to a new encoder family rather than continuing local optimizer search.

recent_performance_findings: round 3: The first-step timeout is caused by serialized DDP startup for local artifacts rather than by the K=128 TopK/Adam configuration itself; allowing both ranks to load the local model and dataset in parallel should get the proxy run to first metrics. -> keep (fvu=0.2119751125574112, observed_fvu=0.220770, k=128, health=normal, termination=completed)
next_move_guidance: If the retry reaches metrics, resume K=128 quality work before considering any lower-K point.
If startup still times out, inspect dataset shuffle/select ordering and other pre-step costs in the local artifact path.
If the run succeeds but Adam does not materially improve K=128 quality, escalate to a new encoder family rather than continuing local optimizer search.
